content.text: Savings Plans

Azure Savings Plans are a flexible commitment-based pricing model that delivers discounts of up to 65% off pay-as-you-go rates in exchange for a fixed hourly spend commitment for one or three years. Unlike Reservations, savings plans are not tied to a specific VM size or Azure region — the discount applies automatically to the cheapest eligible resource in your account each hour.

Introduced in 2022, savings plans address the main limitation of Reservations for teams running heterogeneous or frequently changing compute fleets. You commit to a spend rate (for example, $5.00/hour), and Azure applies the discount to every eligible resource until that hourly budget is consumed.

attrs.alt: Azure Savings Plans commitment flow diagram

content.text: Plan Types
Two savings plan types exist, each covering different Azure services:
Plan Type
Covered Services
Max Discount
Compute Savings Plan
Virtual Machines, Azure Kubernetes Service nodes, Azure Functions Premium, App Service Premium
Up to 65%
Machine Learning Savings Plan
Azure Machine Learning compute clusters and compute instances
Up to 35%

The Compute Savings Plan is the most commonly used. It applies across all VM sizes, OS types, and Azure regions in your scope — the billing engine selects the cheapest eligible resource to consume the discount each hour.
Commitment Terms and Billing Options
Savings plans are available in 1-year and 3-year terms. Within each term you choose how to pay:
Term
Billing Option
Typical Discount
Best For
1 Year
Monthly or Upfront
Up to 37%
Moderate confidence in spend level
3 Year
Monthly or Upfront
Up to 65%
Long-running stable workloads
Upfront payment locks in a slightly higher discount than monthly in some regions. For enterprise cost planning, monthly payment preserves cash flow at the cost of a few percentage points of discount.
Scope: Single Subscription vs Shared
When purchasing a savings plan you specify the scope, which determines which resources are eligible to consume the discount:
Single subscription: discount applies only to resources in the chosen subscription. Useful when a subscription is fully owned by one team or product.
Shared (Management Group): discount applies across all subscriptions under the management group. Maximizes utilization when multiple subscriptions share compute.
Shared (Billing Account/MCA/EA): broadest scope. Azure finds the cheapest eligible resource across all subscriptions in the billing account each hour.

Shared scope produces the highest utilization and best ROI because Azure has the largest pool of resources to apply the discount to. Use single-subscription scope only when cost chargeback requires isolating the savings to one team.
Purchasing a Savings Plan
Savings plans can be purchased through the Azure Portal (Cost Management + Billing -> Savings Plans), or via CLI and API.

# Purchase a 1-year Compute Savings Plan with $5/hr commitment
# (Shared scope, monthly billing)
az savings-plan purchase \
  --billing-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --display-name "compute-sp-prod-1yr" \
  --term P1Y \
  --billing-plan P1M \
  --commitment amount=5.0 currencyCode=USD \
  --applied-scope-type Shared \
  --sku-name Compute_Savings_Plan

# Purchase a 3-year ML Savings Plan with $2/hr commitment, upfront
az savings-plan purchase \
  --billing-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --display-name "ml-sp-3yr-upfront" \
  --term P3Y \
  --billing-plan Upfront \
  --commitment amount=2.0 currencyCode=USD \
  --applied-scope-type Single \
  --applied-scopes /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --sku-name AzureMachineLearning_Savings_Plan

# List all savings plans in the subscription
az savings-plan list --output table

# View details of a specific savings plan
az savings-plan show \
  --savings-plan-order-id <order-id> \
  --savings-plan-id <plan-id>
Monitoring Utilization

The benefit utilization percentage measures how much of your hourly commitment was consumed by eligible resources. A utilization of 100% means your entire committed $/hr was offset by discounted usage. Below 100% means you paid for commitment that had no eligible resource to absorb it that hour — those dollars are lost.

# View savings plan utilization summary (Azure Portal recommended)
# Via CLI: consumption APIs require billing account scope
az consumption savings-plan-utilization list \
  --billing-account-id <billing-account-id> \
  --grain Daily \
  --start-date 2024-06-01 \
  --end-date 2024-06-30 \
  --output table

import datetime
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.consumption import ConsumptionManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ConsumptionManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

# Query savings plan utilization for the last 30 days
end = datetime.date.today()
start = end - datetime.timedelta(days=30)

# Note: full utilization data requires billing account scope (MCA/EA)
# For individual subscriptions, check Azure Cost Management Portal
print(f"Checking utilization from {start} to {end}")
print("Tip: For detailed utilization reports, use Cost Management -> Savings Plans -> Utilization in the Portal")
print("Target >= 90% utilization for healthy ROI. Low utilization means over-committed $/hr.")

Azure Monitor does not expose savings plan utilization as a native metric. Export Cost Management data to a Storage Account or Event Hub and query it with Azure Data Explorer or Power BI for automated alerts.
Right-Sizing Your Commitment
The commitment amount ($/hr) is the most important parameter to get right. Too high and you waste money on unused commitment. Too low and you leave potential savings on the table.
Sizing process
Export your last 30-90 days of compute usage from Cost Management.
Identify the consistent baseline hourly compute spend (the floor, not the peak).
Set commitment to 80-90% of that baseline to stay safely above 90% utilization.
Cover spiky or unpredictable compute with pay-as-you-go; do not try to commit the peak.
Re-evaluate every 6 months and purchase additional plans as stable baseline grows.

# Export the last 90 days of compute usage by day to estimate baseline
az costmanagement export create \
  --name "compute-baseline-export" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --type Usage \
  --time-period fiscalYear \
  --recurrence Daily \
  --recurrence-period from=2024-01-01 to=2024-12-31 \
  --storage-account-id /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/<sa> \
  --destination-container cost-exports \
  --destination-root-folder usage

# After export, query the CSV for compute lines
# Filter: ServiceName = "Virtual Machines" or "Azure Kubernetes Service"

import csv, statistics

# After downloading the Cost Management export CSV
def estimate_commitment(csv_path, percentile=20):
    """Estimate hourly commitment from daily usage data.
    Uses a low percentile to find the stable baseline floor.
    """
    daily_totals = {}
    with open(csv_path) as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            svc = row.get("ServiceName", "")
            if svc in ("Virtual Machines", "Azure Kubernetes Service"):
                date = row["Date"]
                cost = float(row.get("PreTaxCost", 0) or 0)
                daily_totals[date] = daily_totals.get(date, 0) + cost

    if not daily_totals:
        print("No compute usage found in export.")
        return

    daily_costs = sorted(daily_totals.values())
    # Convert daily to hourly
    hourly = [c / 24 for c in daily_costs]
    floor = statistics.quantiles(hourly, n=10)[0]  # 10th percentile
    recommended = round(floor * 0.85, 2)  # 85% of floor for safety margin
    print(f"P10 hourly compute cost: ${floor:.2f}")
    print(f"Recommended commitment: ${recommended:.2f}/hr")
    print(f"Estimated annual savings at 37% (1yr): ${recommended * 8760 * 0.37:.0f}")
    print(f"Estimated annual savings at 65% (3yr): ${recommended * 8760 * 0.65:.0f}")

estimate_commitment("cost-export.csv")
Savings Plans vs Reservations: Choosing the Right Model

Both savings plans and reservations offer commitment discounts, but they differ in flexibility and maximum discount depth. Understanding the tradeoffs helps you decide which to use — or whether to combine both.
Factor
Savings Plans
Reservations
Commitment type
$/hour spend
Specific resource SKU
Flexibility
Any eligible compute, any region
Fixed SKU, OS, region
Max discount (compute)
Up to 65%
Up to 72%
Scope
Single sub or shared
Single sub, shared, or MG
Exchange policy
Cannot exchange
Exchange within product family
Cancellation
Up to $50K/year refund cap
Up to $50K/year refund cap
Portability on resize
Automatic — discount moves to new size
Manual exchange required
Best for
Heterogeneous, dynamic fleets
Stable, known-SKU workloads

In practice many organizations use both: reservations for well-known, stable VM families and savings plans to cover the rest of the fleet. The billing engine applies reservation discounts first, then savings plan discounts on remaining usage.

# Check what is currently covered by reservations vs savings plans
# This shows reservation utilization summary
az consumption reservation summary list \
  --billing-account-id <billing-account-id> \
  --grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2024-06-30 \
  --output table

# Check savings plan utilization (Portal -> Cost Management -> Savings Plans -> Utilization)
# Or export amortized cost data to see benefit allocation per resource
Managing Savings Plans with the Python SDK
The Azure Python SDK (azure-mgmt-billing) provides programmatic access to savings plan lifecycle management: list, read details, and update scope or renewal settings.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.billing import BillingManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
# BillingManagementClient at billing account scope
billing_client = BillingManagementClient(credential)

# List all savings plan orders
def list_savings_plan_orders(billing_account_name: str):
    orders = billing_client.savings_plan_orders.list_by_billing_account(
        billing_account_name=billing_account_name
    )
    results = []
    for order in orders:
        results.append({
            "order_id": order.name,
            "display_name": order.display_name,
            "term": order.term,
            "billing_plan": order.billing_plan,
            "status": order.provisioning_state,
        })
    return results

# List all savings plans within an order
def list_savings_plans(billing_account_name: str, savings_plan_order_id: str):
    plans = billing_client.savings_plans.list_by_savings_plan_order(
        billing_account_name=billing_account_name,
        savings_plan_order_id=savings_plan_order_id
    )
    for plan in plans:
        print(f"Plan: {plan.display_name}")
        print(f"  SKU: {plan.sku.name}")
        print(f"  Status: {plan.provisioning_state}")
        if plan.commitment:
            print(f"  Commitment: {plan.commitment.amount} {plan.commitment.currency_code}/hr")
        if plan.benefit_start_time:
            print(f"  Started: {plan.benefit_start_time}")
        if plan.expiry_date_time:
            print(f"  Expires: {plan.expiry_date_time}")

# Run
orders = list_savings_plan_orders("<billing-account-name>")
for o in orders:
    print(f"Order: {o['display_name']} ({o['term']}, {o['status']})")
    list_savings_plans("<billing-account-name>", o["order_id"])

# Update a savings plan: enable auto-renewal
from azure.mgmt.billing.models import SavingsPlanUpdateRequest, RenewPropertiesResponse

update_request = SavingsPlanUpdateRequest(
    renew=True,  # auto-renew at expiry
    applied_scope_type="Shared",
)

billing_client.savings_plans.update(
    billing_account_name="<billing-account-name>",
    savings_plan_order_id="<order-id>",
    savings_plan_id="<plan-id>",
    body=update_request,
)
print("Auto-renewal enabled.")
Combining Savings Plans with Reserved Instances in CI/CD

In a GitOps or infrastructure-as-code workflow, you track commitment purchases as code alongside your Bicep/Terraform definitions. The following example shows a GitHub Actions workflow that purchases a savings plan when a new environment is provisioned and the baseline spend exceeds a threshold.

# .github/workflows/purchase-savings-plan.yml
name: Purchase Savings Plan

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      commitment_per_hour:
        description: 'Hourly commitment amount in USD'
        required: true
        default: '5.00'
      term:
        description: 'Term: P1Y or P3Y'
        required: true
        default: 'P1Y'
      billing_scope:
        description: 'Billing subscription or account scope'
        required: true

jobs:
  purchase:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Azure Login
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Purchase Savings Plan
        run: |
          az savings-plan purchase \
            --billing-scope "${{ github.event.inputs.billing_scope }}" \
            --display-name "auto-sp-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
            --term "${{ github.event.inputs.term }}" \
            --billing-plan P1M \
            --commitment amount=${{ github.event.inputs.commitment_per_hour }} currencyCode=USD \
            --applied-scope-type Shared \
            --sku-name Compute_Savings_Plan

      - name: Verify Purchase
        run: az savings-plan list --output table
Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
Pitfall
What Happens
How to Avoid
Over-committing $/hr
You pay for commitment hours that have no eligible resource to absorb
Size to 80-90% of P10 baseline hourly spend
Buying savings plan when reservations fit better
Lower discount depth than possible
Use reservations for stable, known VM SKUs; savings plans for the rest
Single-subscription scope with a shared fleet
Utilization is low because the discount cannot reach other subscriptions
Use Shared scope for billing accounts with multiple subscriptions
Ignoring auto-renewal
Plan expires and PAYG rates resume without warning
Enable renew=True or add a budget alert 60 days before expiry
Mixing savings plans and wrong SKU reservations
Billing engine applies in wrong order; harder to audit
Document which services are covered by each commitment type
Purchasing before workload is stable
Commitment outpaces actual usage as workload is still growing
Wait until 90+ days of stable production usage before committing
Tracking Amortized vs Actual Cost
Azure Cost Management exposes two cost views for commitment purchases:
Actual cost: shows the full commitment fee in the period it was paid (lump sum for upfront plans). Useful for budgeting and cash flow.
Amortized cost: spreads the commitment cost evenly across the benefit period and attributes it to the resources that consumed the discount. Useful for chargeback and true per-resource cost analysis.

# Query amortized cost for a resource group (shows per-resource savings plan attribution)
az costmanagement query \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg-name> \
  --type AmortizedCost \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period from=2024-06-01 to=2024-06-30 \
  --dataset-granularity Daily \
  --dataset-grouping name=ResourceGroupName type=Dimension \
                     name=ChargeType type=Dimension \
  --output table

# ChargeType will show "UnusedSavingsPlan" for wasted commitment hours

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.costmanagement import CostManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.costmanagement.models import (
    QueryDefinition, QueryTimePeriod, QueryDataset,
    QueryGrouping, QueryAggregation
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = CostManagementClient(credential)

scope = "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"

query = QueryDefinition(
    type="AmortizedCost",
    timeframe="Custom",
    time_period=QueryTimePeriod(from_property="2024-06-01", to="2024-06-30"),
    dataset=QueryDataset(
        granularity="Daily",
        grouping=[
            QueryGrouping(type="Dimension", name="ChargeType"),
            QueryGrouping(type="Dimension", name="ServiceName"),
        ],
        aggregation={"totalCost": QueryAggregation(name="Cost", function="Sum")},
    ),
)

result = client.query.usage(scope=scope, parameters=query)

unused_total = 0.0
for row in result.rows:
    cost, charge_type, service_name = row[0], row[2], row[3]
    if charge_type == "UnusedSavingsPlan":
        unused_total += cost
        print(f"Wasted savings plan: {service_name} = ${cost:.2f}")

print(f"Total unused commitment this period: ${unused_total:.2f}")
UnusedSavingsPlan rows in amortized cost indicate you over-committed. If these rows appear consistently, reduce your next plan's commitment amount.
Setting Budget Alerts Tied to Savings Plan Coverage
A savings plan reduces your cost per resource but does not cap your total spend. You still need budget alerts to catch unexpected usage spikes above your committed $/hr threshold.

# Create a budget alert for a subscription (monthly, $5000 cap)
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "monthly-compute-budget" \
  --amount 5000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-07-01 \
  --end-date 2025-06-30 \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --notifications \
    enabled=true \
    operator=GreaterThan \
    threshold=80 \
    contactEmails=["ops@example.com"] \
    thresholdType=Actual

# Alert at 80% of budget consumed — gives time to investigate before exceeding limit

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.consumption import ConsumptionManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.consumption.models import (
    Budget, BudgetTimePeriod, BudgetFilter, Notification
)

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ConsumptionManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

budget = Budget(
    category="Cost",
    amount=5000,
    time_grain="Monthly",
    time_period=BudgetTimePeriod(
        start_date="2024-07-01T00:00:00Z",
        end_date="2025-06-30T00:00:00Z",
    ),
    notifications={
        "actual_80": Notification(
            enabled=True,
            operator="GreaterThan",
            threshold=80,
            threshold_type="Actual",
            contact_emails=["ops@example.com"],
        ),
        "forecast_100": Notification(
            enabled=True,
            operator="GreaterThan",
            threshold=100,
            threshold_type="Forecasted",
            contact_emails=["ops@example.com", "finance@example.com"],
        ),
    },
)

client.budgets.create_or_update(
    scope=f"/subscriptions/<subscription-id>",
    budget_name="monthly-compute-budget",
    parameters=budget,
)
print("Budget alert created with 80% actual and 100% forecasted thresholds.")
Exporting Savings Plan Benefits for Chargeback
In a multi-team environment, you need to attribute the savings plan discount to the teams whose resources consumed it. Azure Cost Management amortized cost export is the standard mechanism for this.

# Create a recurring amortized cost export to a storage account
az costmanagement export create \
  --name "amortized-monthly-chargeback" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --type AmortizedCost \
  --time-period fiscalYear \
  --recurrence Monthly \
  --recurrence-period from=2024-01-01 to=2025-12-31 \
  --storage-account-id /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/<sa> \
  --destination-container cost-amortized \
  --destination-root-folder chargeback

import csv, collections

# After downloading the amortized cost export CSV:
# Compute savings plan benefit per team tag
def chargeback_report(csv_path):
    by_team = collections.defaultdict(lambda: {"payg_cost": 0.0, "amortized_cost": 0.0, "savings": 0.0})
    with open(csv_path) as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            team = row.get("TagTeam", "untagged")
            # PayGPrice * Quantity is the undiscounted cost
            payg = float(row.get("PayGPrice", 0) or 0) * float(row.get("Quantity", 0) or 0)
            amort = float(row.get("CostInBillingCurrency", 0) or 0)
            by_team[team]["payg_cost"] += payg
            by_team[team]["amortized_cost"] += amort
            by_team[team]["savings"] += (payg - amort)

    print(f"{'Team':<20} {'PAYG Cost':>12} {'Amortized':>12} {'Savings':>12} {'Savings %':>10}")
    print("-" * 70)
    for team, d in sorted(by_team.items()):
        pct = (d['savings'] / d['payg_cost'] * 100) if d['payg_cost'] > 0 else 0
        print(f"{team:<20} ${d['payg_cost']:>11,.2f} ${d['amortized_cost']:>11,.2f} ${d['savings']:>11,.2f} {pct:>9.1f}%")

chargeback_report("amortized-export.csv")

Teams with low savings percentages may have resources that are not eligible for the savings plan (PaaS services outside the coverage scope) or are running in regions where the plan was not applied. Investigate per-resource ChargeType in the export.
Summary

Azure Savings Plans provide flexible, commitment-based discounts of up to 65% off pay-as-you-go compute rates without tying you to a specific VM size or region. Choose Compute Savings Plans for broad VM/AKS/Functions coverage and Machine Learning Savings Plans for AML workloads. Size your hourly commitment to 80-90% of your stable compute spend baseline, use shared scope for maximum utilization, and re-evaluate every 6 months as your workload grows.
